assets.name: lecture_04_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the best balance of completeness and clarity. It preserves the already-written polar-coordinate Lagrangian and small angle sketch at the top, while also showing the new partial-derivative expression in a more developed state than the earlier candidates. The later frame has a slightly cleaner fraction bar, but the derivative itself is less advanced.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: We first construct the derivative of the Lagrangian with respect

assets.name: lecture_04_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: This frame is the clearest and most usable for notes. It keeps the already-written canonical-momentum definition visible at the top and shows the new lower line, d\pi_i/dt equals, in a stable and unobstructed state. The later frames nominally advance the right-hand side, but Susskind's hand covers too much of the fresh writing to make them better evidence.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: equal to the derivative of the Lagrangian
assets.caption_hint: canonical momentum and its time derivative

assets.name: lecture_04_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: This frame keeps the main formula A = \int \mathcal{L}\,dt clearly visible, retains the stationarity condition \delta A = 0 above it, and also preserves the nearby curved path sketch on the left. It is the most informative of the four because the lower line has advanced to a second integral expression, while the central action formula remains unobscured.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: the action is equal to the integral.
assets.caption_hint: action as the integral of the Lagrangian

assets.name: lecture_04_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the clearest overall board view with the least occlusion. It keeps the full layout intact: the trajectory sketch on the left, the stationarity condition and action definition at the top, and the variation formula across the center. The neighboring candidates are similar, but this one is the most balanced and readable for note writing.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: starting trajectory being a solution of the equations of motion
